item.title: boarding school sexual abuse lawsuits in Missouri and surrounding states — institutional liability for residential schools
item.description:
I notice the “article” you’ve submitted is actually my previous refusal to keyword-stuff an unrelated topic into a Title IX survivor article. There’s no article here to polish.

Here’s what I can do right now:

If you have the actual boarding school sexual abuse / Title IX article, paste it and I’ll do the final editorial pass as requested — cutting filler, sharpening the HB68 references, tightening calls to action, and stripping any AI tells until it reads like it came from a lawyer who has spent two decades deposing school administrators.

item.title: Coach Sexual Abuse in Missouri Youth Sports: School District Liability, Title IX Rights, and Legal Options for Survivors
item.description:
Coach Sexual Abuse in Missouri Youth Sports: School District Liability, Title IX Rights, and Legal Options for Survivors

Legal Information for Missouri Survivors and Families

⚠️ CRITICAL DEADLINE WARNING FOR MISSOURI SURVIVORS Missouri’s statute of limitations for childhood sexual abuse claims has changed. If you or your child experienced coach sexual abuse in a Missouri school sports program, your deadline to file a civil lawsuit may be running right now — and once it expires, it cannot be extended, waived, or recovered. Waiting is not a neutral choice. Every day without legal counsel is a day your rights may be narrowing. Read this article, then call a Missouri Title IX attorney today.

item.title: Missouri Schools Failed Your Child. Here's What the Law Gives You Now.
item.description:
Missouri Schools Failed Your Child. Here’s What the Law Gives You Now.

Missouri’s HB 68 changed everything for survivors of school sexual abuse. If a teacher, coach, administrator, or staff member abused your child — or if it happened years ago and you stayed silent because you thought the window had closed — that law may have just reopened the door to justice.

Here’s what you need to know.

What Schools Are Required to Do — and What They Actually Do

Missouri schools operate under two parallel legal frameworks: federal Title IX and state mandatory reporting law. Both impose hard obligations on school employees. Both get violated constantly.

item.title: Religious School and Church-Affiliated School Sexual Abuse in Missouri: Accountability and Civil Claims for Survivors
item.description:
Religious School and Church-Affiliated School Sexual Abuse in Missouri: Accountability and Civil Claims for Survivors

If Your Child Was Abused at a Religious or Church-Affiliated School in Missouri, You Have Legal Rights

Sexual abuse by teachers, coaches, priests, ministers, youth directors, and other trusted adults at religious and church-affiliated schools causes profound, lasting harm. Survivors in Missouri face a particularly difficult road to accountability because these institutions operate at the intersection of religious autonomy claims, private school governance structures, and civil liability rules that differ meaningfully from those governing public schools. Knowing exactly which laws apply — and where institutions failed their legal obligations — is the foundation of any viable claim.

item.title: Student-on-Student Sexual Assault in Missouri Schools: Title IX Liability and Your Rights as a Survivor
item.description:
Student-on-Student Sexual Assault in Missouri Schools: Title IX Liability and Your Rights as a Survivor

What Missouri Families Need to Know About School District Accountability for Peer Sexual Abuse

⚠ CRITICAL DEADLINE WARNING FOR MISSOURI SURVIVORS

Missouri law governs the time you have to file a civil lawsuit for school sexual assault. These deadlines are absolute — once they pass, your right to compensation is permanently and irrevocably gone. No court can extend them. No exception applies because your child is still in school, because you are still in counseling, or because the criminal case is unresolved. If your child was sexually assaulted at a Missouri school, the legal clock is running right now, today, while you read this. Call a Missouri Title IX attorney today — not next week, not after the school year ends.

item.title: Suing a Missouri School District for Sexual Abuse: How Civil Lawsuits Work and What Damages You Can Recover
item.description:
Suing a Missouri School District for Sexual Abuse: How Civil Lawsuits Work and What Damages You Can Recover

Legal information for Missouri survivors and families — not legal advice. If you or your child experienced sexual abuse, assault, or harassment at a Missouri school, contact a qualified Title IX attorney before taking any other steps.

⚠️ CRITICAL DEADLINE WARNING FOR MISSOURI SURVIVORS

Missouri House Bill 68, enacted in April 2025, fundamentally changed the statute of limitations for certain Missouri civil claims. Depending on your situation, you may have only months — not years — to file before your right to any recovery is permanently extinguished. There are no exceptions. There is no way to reopen a case after the deadline passes.

item.description:

item.title: When Missouri Schools Fail to Protect Students from Sexual Abuse: Title IX, Deliberate Indifference, and Survivor Rights
item.description:
When Missouri Schools Fail to Protect Students from Sexual Abuse: Title IX, Deliberate Indifference, and Survivor Rights

Your child told a teacher. The teacher told the principal. The principal told you it was being handled. Then nothing happened — and the abuse continued. Or the coach who touched students was quietly transferred from Rockwood School District to Wentzville School District. Or the University of Missouri dismissed your daughter’s rape report because she and her attacker had been dating. If any of this happened to your family in a Missouri school, you may be looking at a civil rights violation, not just a tragedy.
